chart-patterns-psx.title: 5 Chart Patterns That Work Best on PSX Stocks
chart-patterns-psx.intro: Open any trading book and you'll find sixty chart patterns. Most of them were catalogued on US stocks in the 1940s and have been recycled ever since. The PSX is a different animal — thinner liquidity, sharper retail behaviour, news-sensitive, and prone to long quiet stretches between bursts. After years of watching KSE-100 charts every single day, I can tell you that only a handful of patterns actually show up cleanly enough to act on. The rest are noise.

sections.heading: 1. Head & Shoulders
sections.body: Picture a stock that has been climbing for months. It pushes to a high, pulls back, makes a higher high (the head), pulls back again to about the level of the first peak, then rolls over. Three peaks, the middle one tallest. The line under the two pullbacks is the neckline. When price closes below that line on real volume, the trend is finished. The bottom version — Inverse Head & Shoulders — works the same in reverse and shows up after long downtrends. On the PSX it's most reliable on index-heavy names where institutions actually trade in size.

sections.heading: 2. Double Top and Double Bottom
sections.body: The simplest pattern in the book and one of the few I trust on PSX. Price hits a level, fails, hits the same level again, fails again. That's a double top — buyers ran out of conviction. Double bottom is the same thing inverted. You'll see it most often at psychological round numbers where retail orders cluster. If you only ever learn one pattern, learn this one.

sections.heading: 3. Triangles
sections.body: Price makes lower highs and higher lows at the same time, squeezing into a corner. That's a triangle. Ascending triangles (flat top, rising bottom) usually break upward — buyers keep paying more while sellers hold a line. Descending is the bearish mirror. Symmetrical is a coin flip until it breaks. PSX stocks consolidate constantly, especially during low-volume sessions, so triangles are the pattern you'll see most often.

sections.heading: 4. Flag and Pennant
sections.body: After a big move, price doesn't usually reverse — it pauses. A flag is a small rectangle that drifts gently against the trend; a pennant is a small triangle. Both say the same thing: the move is taking a breath. They show up reliably during PSX result-season rallies, when a strong earnings print drives a stock up sharply and it then chops sideways for a few days before pushing higher.

sections.heading: 5. Cup and Handle
sections.body: The slowest of the bunch and my personal favourite. A stock spends months grinding through a rounded bottom (the cup), recovers most of the prior decline, drifts sideways for a few weeks (the handle), then breaks to new highs. It takes patience to wait for one, but when a KSE-100 leader prints a clean cup and handle coming out of a long base, the move that follows is usually worth more than ten flags.

chart-patterns-psx.teaser: Spotting the pattern is the easy part. The hard part — and the part that decides whether you make money or hand it back — is when to actually pull the trigger. Where the entry sits relative to the breakout. Where the stop goes. What volume needs to look like for the pattern to be real. Which timeframes you can trust and which lie. That is the work, and it's what we cover in the Technical Analysis course with real PSX charts pulled apart in front of you.

pkr-usd-psx.title: Why the PKR/USD Rate is the Most Powerful PSX Indicator
pkr-usd-psx.intro: Pakistan buys more from the world than it sells to the world. Hold that one fact in your head and most of the macro picture starts to make sense. The rupee-to-dollar exchange rate is not just a headline number — it is the single biggest lever moving Pakistani company earnings, sector by sector. Every meaningful move in the rupee redistributes profits across the listed market. Most retail investors panic when the rupee drops. The traders who actually make money treat it as a reshuffle of which stocks to own.

sections.heading: When the Rupee Falls, Half the Market Wins
sections.body: If you run a textile mill in Faisalabad, your customers are in Europe and the US. They pay you in dollars. When those dollars convert to more rupees, your reported profit goes up — without you doing anything. Same story for IT exporters, surgical instrument makers, basmati rice exporters, leather goods. A weaker rupee is free margin for them. The market knows this and reprices these stocks fast — sometimes within hours of a sharp rupee move.

sections.heading: And the Other Half Loses
sections.body: The flip side is just as predictable. Auto assemblers import parts. Pharma companies import APIs. Oil marketing companies sell fuel that came from somewhere else. Every one of those businesses sends dollars out — and when those dollars cost more rupees, their margins compress. If you're holding a portfolio that is long importers during a rupee depreciation cycle, you are fighting the macro current.

sections.heading: The Mistake Most People Make
sections.body: Open Pakistani news on any rupee-falling day and you'll see the political angle, the IMF angle, the inflation angle. What you'll rarely see is the equity angle, which is exactly the one that matters if you're trying to grow your money. A 2% rupee move is a routine event for the textile sector and a margin alarm for the auto sector. Stop reading rupee news as politics. Start reading it as a sector signal.

pkr-usd-psx.teaser: Knowing the direction is step one. Sizing it correctly, identifying which exporter actually benefits and which one is hedged, separating the real currency-driven move from the political noise around it, and timing the entry against a clean technical setup — that is what turns a vague macro view into a real PSX trade. The Fundamental Analysis course walks through the full sector map and the data sources I check every morning.

sbp-interest-rates.title: How SBP Interest Rate Decisions Move the Stock Market
sbp-interest-rates.intro: Eight times a year the Monetary Policy Committee at the State Bank meets, deliberates, and announces the policy rate. The number lands at 5pm on a Monday. By Tuesday morning, the KSE-100 has reshuffled. If you've ever watched cement, steel, and auto stocks rip in the days after a rate cut while everyone else stays flat, you've watched this in real time without anyone explaining it to you. Here's the explanation.

sections.heading: What the Rate Actually Is
sections.body: The policy rate is the price of money in the country. When SBP cuts it, banks borrow cheaper, lend cheaper, and businesses can finance projects at lower cost. The future earnings of every listed company are also valued more highly when interest rates are low — because the discount used to compare future cash to today's cash gets smaller. So a rate cut does two things at once: it makes companies more profitable, and it makes the market value those profits more generously.

sections.heading: Who Wins, in What Order
sections.body: Not every sector reacts the same. Highly leveraged businesses — cement, steel, autos, textiles with big debt loads — get the biggest tailwind because their interest cost drops directly. Banks have a more complicated reaction: their margins compress in the short run, but loan demand and asset quality improve, so they often catch up later. Power, fertiliser, and defensive cash-rich companies barely move on rate decisions because they were never financed by debt in the first place.

sections.heading: Why Most People Miss the Move
sections.body: By the time the rate decision is announced, most of the move has already happened. The market reads the inflation print, listens to the SBP governor's interviews, watches the bond yields, and prices in the most likely outcome days before the meeting. If you wait for the press release to enter, you're paying full price for a position the smart money already opened. Reading what's coming is more valuable than reacting to what just happened.

sbp-interest-rates.teaser: The mechanics are simple to write down and hard to apply. Knowing which PSX sector leads, which lags by two weeks, when consensus is wrong about the next decision, and how to combine the rate view with a technical entry — that is the part I walk students through every cycle in the Fundamental & Macro module. The framework is repeatable, and once you've sat through one cycle with it, you stop guessing.

dow-theory-kse.title: Dow Theory Applied to KSE-100: A Practical Guide
dow-theory-kse.intro: Charles Dow died in 1902. He never saw a candlestick chart, never heard the word algorithm, and certainly never traded the KSE-100. But the framework that grew out of his editorials still describes how markets move better than most modern indicators. There is a reason it survived a hundred and twenty years. On the PSX, where retail behaviour is loud and a small number of institutional players drive most of the real money, Dow's framework is one of the cleanest filters you can apply.

sections.heading: Three Trends, All Happening at Once
sections.body: Every market is moving in three timeframes simultaneously. The primary trend is the big one — months to years. The secondary trend is the correction inside it, lasting weeks to a couple of months. The minor trend is the day-to-day chop. Most retail traders zoom in on the chop, take dozens of small losses, and wonder why the people who barely seem to trade keep ending the year ahead of them. The answer is they're trading a different timeframe. They picked the tide. The retail trader is fighting the ripples.

sections.heading: The Three Phases of a Bull Market
sections.body: Dow noticed that bull markets unfold in roughly three phases. First is accumulation, when the news is still bad and the smart money is buying quietly because valuations are absurdly cheap. Second is public participation — the trend becomes obvious, charts look great, retail joins, this is where the biggest gains happen. Third is distribution, when the news is euphoric and the smart money is selling into strength. Bear markets do the same thing in reverse. The phase you're in determines the trade.

sections.heading: Confirmation
sections.body: Dow's original work demanded that two indices confirm each other before he'd believe a trend change. On the PSX you can adapt this idea: when the KSE-100 makes a new high, you want to see breadth confirming it — the number of stocks above their 50-day moving average, the volume profile, the leading sectors all moving together. An index move with no breadth behind it is a warning, not a buy signal.

dow-theory-kse.teaser: The theory takes ten minutes to read. Applying it without lying to yourself — calling the phase correctly, reading volume right, refusing to call the top before it has actually printed — that is years of practice or a structured course that compresses the years. The Technical Analysis course walks through Dow Theory on real KSE-100 charts month by month, and shows you the mistakes I made early on so you don't repeat them.

balance-of-trade.title: Reading Pakistan's Balance of Trade for Stock Opportunities
balance-of-trade.intro: Once a month the Pakistan Bureau of Statistics releases the trade data — what the country bought from the world, what it sold, and what the difference looks like. It is one of the most important documents for anyone trying to invest seriously on the PSX, and it is also one of the least read. Buried inside that PDF is a sector signal that hands you opportunities most retail investors will never even know existed.

sections.heading: The Headline Number
sections.body: Trade balance is exports minus imports. Pakistan almost always runs a deficit — we import more than we export — which is why everyone obsesses over how big the gap is. A widening deficit drains foreign exchange reserves, pressures the rupee, and eventually forces the State Bank's hand on rates. A narrowing deficit gives the country breathing room and tends to coincide with strong PSX runs. The number itself matters less than which way it's moving.

sections.heading: Read it as a Sector Map
sections.body: Don't stop at the headline. Look at the components. Strong textile exports are a green light for the textile sector for the next few weeks. Strong IT exports support tech-services names. A heavy oil import month — which happens when global crude rallies — is a yellow flag for OMCs and refineries because their working capital balloons and margins squeeze. Once you start reading the breakdown instead of the front-page deficit, the report becomes a weekly to-do list.

sections.heading: Trend, Not the Print
sections.body: A single month is noise. Numbers bounce because of one-off shipments, holidays, or accounting timing. The signal is in the rolling three-month average and the year-on-year comparison — that is what FX dealers and serious analysts watch. If you react to the headline of one bad month, you'll be selling into a bottom that the trend would have warned you was about to reverse.

balance-of-trade.teaser: Reading the report is one piece. Layering it onto FX reserves, REER, remittance flows, and the SBP's positioning to anticipate which sector is about to get its move — that is what the Fundamental & Macro course walks through. I'll show you the exact data sources I open every Monday morning, the order I read them in, and how I translate them into a watchlist by Tuesday.

position-sizing.title: The Position Sizing Formula That Kept My Max Drawdown at -3.3%
position-sizing.intro: Across 518 trades over twelve months, my maximum drawdown was -3.3%. That's not because I picked stocks better than everyone else. Plenty of traders pick well. It's because I sized them correctly. Position sizing is the most under-taught and most decisive variable in trading — full stop. You can be right 60% of the time and still blow up an account if you put too much money on a single bad call. You can be right 40% of the time and still come out ahead if you size properly. This is the most important article on this site.

sections.heading: What Position Sizing Actually Means
sections.body: Forget how many shares to buy. That's not the question. The real question is this: how much money are you willing to lose on this trade if it goes against you? Not in your gut — in actual rupees. Once you have a clear answer to that, the share count is just arithmetic. Risk first, size second. Most retail traders do the opposite: they pick a share count that feels right and discover the loss only after it's hit them.

sections.heading: The 1% Rule
sections.body: A reasonable starting principle for almost every retail trader: never risk more than 1% of your trading capital on a single trade. If you're trading with PKR 1,000,000, that's PKR 10,000 of pain you can absorb if your stop is hit. Some experienced traders go to 2%. Beginners should sit closer to 0.5%. The point is not the exact number — it's that the number exists, in writing, before you place the trade. If you don't have one, the market will pick one for you, and you won't like it.

sections.heading: Why Most Traders Skip This
sections.body: Position sizing feels like a brake. You're excited about a trade, you've done the work, you're sure — and the rule says risk only 1%. It feels small. It feels like leaving money on the table. Then the loss you weren't expecting arrives and the rule pays for itself ten times over. The traders who survive long enough to actually compound their capital are the ones who internalised this before their first big drawdown, not after. Once after, it's too late.

position-sizing.teaser: The 1% rule is the floor. Above it sits a whole framework: how to scale up after winning streaks, how to scale down during drawdowns, how to set portfolio-wide risk limits so correlated PSX positions don't all blow up on the same day, and how to size dynamically against volatility. The full methodology — including the actual spreadsheet I use to track every trade — is walked through in the Risk module of the Technical Analysis course.
